The al-Faisal family was on their way to visit relatives in a city three hours from Riyadh when the sky began to change colour. Faisal, sitting in the back seat, noticed it first. "Baba," he said, "why is it turning orange?"

Abu Faisal looked at the horizon and immediately understood. A severe sandstorm was approaching from the desert to the west. In the thirty years he had lived in Saudi Arabia, he had seen dozens of them, but they still commanded respect. The phenomenon could be beautiful and terrifying at the same time.

He pulled the car to the side of the road and stopped. Around them, other drivers were doing the same. Visibility was dropping fast. Within ten minutes, the world outside the windows had become a dense, swirling mass of orange dust, through which the shapes of other parked vehicles were barely visible. The particles of sand striking the car made a sound like quiet, continuous rain.

The family sat together in the warm car and waited. Umm Faisal distributed water and dates from the bag she always brought for road trips. Faisal pressed his face close to the window and tried to see through the dust.

Jaddah told them a story from her childhood about a sandstorm that had lasted three days and had changed the shape of the land around her family's farm in Qassim. When the wind finally stopped, she said, the dunes had moved and the paths between fields had disappeared. The community had had to rediscover their own land.

"Were you frightened?" Faisal asked her.

"We were prepared," Jaddah said. "That is the regional way. You do not fight the desert. You understand it."

The storm passed within an hour. The disrupt to their journey was only a delay. Abu Faisal started the engine again, and the family continued down the road. The landscape on either side was now completely covered in a thin, pale skin of fresh sand, and everything looked new.

family.variant_title: Faisal's Memory Palace at Home
family.variant_body:
Ten-year-old Faisal had a big school test coming up. He needed to remember fifteen difficult words in Arabic, ten important dates in Islamic history, and the names of all the regions of Saudi Arabia. His mother, Umm Faisal, watched him read his notes over and over again and saw the frustration building on his face.

"It's not going in," he said. "I read it and then it disappears."

His grandfather, Jaddah, was sitting in the majlis and overheard. "Come here," he said quietly. "I will teach you something that scholars have used for a thousand years."

The technique is called the memory palace. You choose a familiar location — your own home, the walk from your school gate to your classroom, the path through the family garden — and you use it to encode new information in a visual way. You associate each piece of information with a specific, vivid image placed at a specific location along your mental route.

Jaddah walked Faisal through the family home in his imagination. At the front door, Faisal placed the image of a soldier — to remember the Battle of Badr. In the living room, he placed a large map — for geography. By the stove in the kitchen, he placed a glowing lamp — for the word "enlightenment." In his father's study, he placed a calendar marked with a bright red date.

"Every location holds something," Jaddah said. "Walk through the house in your mind, and everything will be there waiting for you."

Faisal tried it that evening. The next morning, he walked through the house in his imagination before breakfast, and everything he had placed there was still exactly where he had left it. When the test came two days later, he was astonished. He could recall every word, every date, and every region without hesitation.

Abu Faisal, his father, was impressed when he heard about the method. He tried it himself for a presentation at work. It worked just as well.

The memory palace, Jaddah told the family at dinner that evening, is not a trick. "It is simply the way a mind works when it is given a home for what it knows."

family.variant_title: The Ancient City the Al-Faisal Family Visited
family.variant_body:
During the school holiday, Abu Faisal drove the family to AlUla. He had wanted to make the trip for years, ever since he had read about the ancient Nabataean city of Hegra — the extraordinary collection of carved tombs and monumental structures cut directly into the red sandstone cliffs of the desert landscape.

When they arrived, Faisal stood in front of the first carved tomb and could not speak for almost a minute. He had seen photographs, but the photographs had not communicated the sheer scale of the thing — the facade rising more than fifteen metres above the desert floor, the precise and beautiful carving of the architectural details, the way the ancient stone glowed in the morning light.

A guide explained the process of excavation that had revealed the site to modern scholars. Over many decades, archaeologists had carefully removed the layer upon layer of sand and debris that had accumulated around the base of the tombs, exposing artefacts that told the story of the Nabataean civilisation in remarkable detail. Coins, pottery, personal ornaments, and written inscriptions had all been found, and each discovery had added to the picture of a sophisticated, cosmopolitan community that had built this extraordinary city two thousand years ago.

Jaddah was particularly moved by the tombs themselves — the thought that real people, with real families, had been buried here, and that their names and the names of their relatives were still carved in the stone above the doors, readable after two thousand years.

Faisal pressed his hand against the warm rock and felt the carved grooves of an inscription beneath his palm. "These are words," he said. "Someone wrote these."

"Yes," Jaddah said. "Someone who wanted to be remembered."

The al-Faisal family drove home the following day. Faisal spent most of the journey drawing the ruins from memory in his notebook. He had a school project on history the following week, and for the first time in his life, he knew exactly what he wanted to write about.

family.canonical_title: Racing Against Time: Saving Earth's Wildlife
family.variant_title: The Family That Went to See the Desert Animals
family.variant_body:
Last spring holiday, the al-Faisal family drove south from Riyadh toward the protected wildlife area at the edge of the Rub' al Khali. Abu Faisal had been reading about the conservation efforts underway in the area, and he wanted Faisal to see it for himself — to understand that Saudi Arabia is home to a rich variety of wild species that are in genuine need of protection.

The drive took five hours. Faisal spent most of it asking questions about the animals they might see. His father explained that the Arabian oryx — once completely extinct in the wild — had been successfully reintroduced through a careful conservation programme. The animal had disappeared from the wild decades earlier, largely because of poaching, and had survived only in captivity. Now, small herds were living in protected areas and their numbers were slowly growing.

When they arrived at the reserve, a ranger led them on a guided walk along a marked route. The habitat was extraordinary — a vast, open landscape with scattered desert shrubs and rock formations that glowed orange in the afternoon light. After about thirty minutes, the ranger stopped and pointed silently to a low ridge.

There were three Arabian oryx standing in the shade of a large rock. They were white, with long straight horns, and they stood completely still, watching the humans below them with calm, dark eyes. Faisal held his breath. He had seen photographs of oryx, but nothing had prepared him for the reality of seeing them — elegant, wild, and completely at home in a landscape that looked, to him, almost impossibly empty.

Jaddah, who had come on the trip despite the heat, stood beside Faisal and put her hand gently on his shoulder. "When I was a child," she said, "my grandfather told me there were oryx in the desert. By the time I grew up, they had disappeared. Seeing them again feels like something that should not have been possible."

Faisal said nothing. He simply watched until the oryx moved away over the ridge and disappeared.

family.variant_title: The Documentary Night That Changed Faisal's Mind
family.variant_body:
Once a month, Abu Faisal organises what he calls "film night" in the family majlis. He chooses a documentary — usually something about nature, history, or the people of the Arab world — and the family watches it together. Jaddah sometimes joins them, though she often falls asleep quietly during the second half.

Last month, the film was about a community of Bedouin families in the northern desert who had been living a semi-nomadic life for generations and who were navigating the changes brought by modern Saudi development. The documentary showed the daily life of the families — their tents, their animals, their food, and their conversations — with great care and without trying to portray them as either primitive or romantic.

Faisal had not wanted to watch it. He had been hoping for something about animals or technology. But within fifteen minutes he was completely absorbed. The film showed a family that was, in many ways, not so different from his own — parents who worried about their children, grandparents who told stories, children who sometimes argued and sometimes helped each other.

What struck him most was a scene in which a young man from the family explained why he had decided to stay with his community rather than move to the city to study. His narrative was calm and clear: he believed that the perspective available from a life connected to the land, to animals, and to a community that depended on mutual support was worth more than any degree. He was not opposed to education or to the wider world. He simply believed that what he was learning where he was could not be learned anywhere else.

Abu Faisal paused the film at this point and asked Faisal what he thought. Faisal was quiet for a moment. He said he had always thought there was only one way to have a good life. He had not considered that this might be a stereotype he had never examined.

"That is what good films do," Abu Faisal said. "They influence you to see beyond what you already know."

family.variant_title: How the Al-Faisal Family Went Digital
family.variant_body:
Two years ago, the al-Faisal family made a decision that surprised several of their friends: they bought a virtual school subscription for Faisal that complemented his regular school lessons. The programme used algorithms to personalise Faisal's learning experience, identifying the areas where he was strong and the areas where he needed more practice, and then automatically adjusting the difficulty and type of content it offered him.

At first, Abu Faisal was sceptical. He had grown up learning from books and teachers, and he was not sure whether a computer programme could really understand what a child needed. But he watched how Faisal used the platform, and he was impressed by the detail and care of its design. The programme monitored his progress over time, celebrated his achievements, and gently redirected him when he was struggling — without ever making him feel that he had failed.

Umm Faisal had a different concern. She worried that too much screen time would reduce Faisal's ability to concentrate without digital stimulation — that the constant feedback and visual engagement of the learning platform would make sitting quietly with a book feel dull by comparison. She monitored his reading habits carefully and made sure that the digital learning was balanced with time spent with physical books, outdoor play, and family conversation.

Jaddah was the most straightforward about her view. She did not use a smartphone, she had no social media, and she did not particularly want to. But she told Faisal that every generation has its tools, and that the important thing is not which tools you use, but what you do with them. "A good consumer of knowledge," she said, "is not defined by the technology they use, but by the questions they ask."

The trend toward digital learning in Saudi schools has continued to grow. Abu Faisal expects that by the time Faisal finishes secondary school, most of his learning will have some digital component. His job as a parent, he believes, is not to resist this, but to make sure that Faisal develops the human skills — curiosity, critical thinking, empathy, patience — that no retailer of technology will ever be able to programme into a machine.

family.variant_title: The New School Faisal Almost Did Not Like
family.variant_body:
When Faisal started at his new school in the autumn, he was not sure about it. The building was very modern — all glass and light and open corridors — and the classrooms were equipped with smart boards, adjustable furniture, and a solar-powered air circulation system that made no noise at all. It was unlike any school he had attended before.

The school had been designed around the principle of sustainable learning environments — the idea that the physical space where children learn has a direct effect on how well they learn. The infrastructure was built to be as efficient as possible: renewable energy sources, natural lighting wherever possible, and an urban garden on the roof where students grew vegetables as part of their science programme.

At first, Faisal missed his old school. He missed the familiar classrooms, the particular smell of the corridors, and the teachers whose voices he knew. The new school felt too large and too clean and too organised. He told his mother that it did not feel like a school — it felt like an airport.

Umm Faisal listened to him carefully and then suggested that he give it three weeks before forming an opinion. "New places always feel strange at first," she said. "That is not the same as wrong."

By the end of the first month, Faisal had changed his mind. The rooftop garden had become his favourite part of the day. The science teacher, who ran the garden programme, had shown him how to integrate what they were growing with what they were learning in class — how the life cycle of a plant connected to topics in biology, how the water management of the garden connected to topics in geography and mathematics.

Abu Faisal visited the school for a parent meeting and was impressed by what he found. The school was not just modern — it was thoughtful. It had been designed with a genuine understanding of how children learn and what they need to thrive. He came home and told Faisal that he agreed with him: it was an exceptional school.

"But it still looks like an airport," Faisal said.

family.variant_title: The Trip to Jaddah's Village
family.variant_body:
Every few years, the al-Faisal family makes a trip that is different from their usual holidays. They do not fly to another country or stay in a large hotel. They drive to the small village in Qassim where Jaddah was born, and they spend four days as close to the way she grew up as they can manage.

The village is quiet. There is no shopping centre nearby and no fast food. The neighbours are old friends of Jaddah's family — people who have known her since childhood and who always welcome the al-Faisal family with great warmth. The pace of life is slower, and the disconnect from the usual rhythms of Riyadh is immediate and noticeable.

Abu Faisal calls these trips a "family retreat." He believes that the physical experience of stepping out of everyday life — even for a few days — produces a quality of contemplation that is impossible to manufacture in a city environment. When you are away from your routines, your usual distractions, and the constant forward momentum of work and school and obligation, you begin to see your real life more clearly.

Faisal has always had complicated feelings about these trips. He finds the early days difficult — he misses his friends, he misses the internet, he misses the familiar rhythms of home. But by the third day, something always shifts. He starts noticing things he never notices in Riyadh: the quality of the silence at night, the specific smell of the air in the morning, the way Jaddah's face looks different here — more relaxed, more at home, more authentic.

On the last evening of this year's trip, the family sat together on the terrace of the old house and watched the stars. Faisal counted more stars than he had ever seen in Riyadh, where the city lights make the sky almost blank.

"This is what it looked like every night when I was your age," Jaddah told him. "Restore this in your memory," she said. "You may need it someday."

He did.

family.variant_title: The Mountain Holiday That Changed How Faisal Saw Saudi Arabia
family.variant_body:
When Abu Faisal announced that the summer holiday would be spent in the mountains of the Asir region, Faisal was not enthusiastic. He had been hoping for a beach holiday. He had never been to Asir, and he had no clear picture of what it would look like.

The expedition took two days of driving. As the family moved south and west, and the altitude gradually increased, Faisal began to notice that the landscape was changing in ways that surprised him. The desert was giving way to something greener and cooler. By the time they reached the higher roads of the Asir highlands, the view from the car windows was unlike anything Faisal had seen in Saudi Arabia before.

The mountains were extraordinary. The ecosystem was completely different from the central plateau around Riyadh — dense shrubs, terraced farms on steep hillsides, waterfalls visible in the distance, and a breathtaking variety of birds and insects that Faisal had never encountered. He pressed his face to the window and stared.

They stayed in a small guesthouse run by a local family. The heritage of the area was evident everywhere: in the architecture of the old stone houses, in the embroidered decorations that the local women made, and in the food — distinctly different from what the family ate at home in Riyadh, richer in the flavours of wild herbs and the region's famous honey.

Abu Faisal hired a local guide to take them on a walk through the mountain terrain. The guide pointed out endemic plant species that grew only at this altitude, explained the traditional farming practices that had shaped the landscape for centuries, and showed them a viewpoint from which the extent of the mountain biodiversity was suddenly clear — valleys of green running down to the distant glimmer of the Red Sea far below.

Faisal came home from the trip with a notebook full of drawings and a completely new understanding of his country. He had not known that Saudi Arabia contained this world. He has already asked whether they can go back next year.

family.variant_title: Abu Faisal's Desert Expedition
family.variant_body:
Last year, Abu Faisal did something that none of his family had expected. He joined a small group of men — all of them fathers, all of them around his age — on a four-day walking expedition through a section of the Nefud desert in northern Saudi Arabia. They carried their own water and food, slept in lightweight tents, and followed a route guided by a desert specialist from a local tribe.

He did not tell Umm Faisal or Faisal until two weeks before he left. When he did, their reactions were different. Umm Faisal was worried about the extreme heat and the physical demands of the terrain. Faisal thought it sounded exciting and immediately asked if he could come.

"Not yet," Abu Faisal said. "But when you are older, we will go together."

The expedition lasted four days. The isolation was complete — no phone signal, no news, no work emails, no responsibilities except walking, eating, and sleeping. The endurance required was real. By the second day, Abu Faisal's feet were blistered and his legs were aching. The group walked mostly in the early morning and the late afternoon, resting during the hottest part of the day in whatever shade they could create.

The navigation through the desert relied on the knowledge of their guide and on the position of the sun. There were no landmarks and no roads — only the rolling landscape of orange and red sand, the enormous sky, and the sound of the wind.

Abu Faisal came home from the trip quieter than usual. He did not talk much about what had happened, and he did not show photographs immediately. It took him a few days to begin finding words for the experience.

He told Faisal eventually that what had surprised him most was how alive he had felt — more present than he was in ordinary life, more aware of each moment, more genuinely grateful for simple things: shade, water, bread, and the company of other people.

Faisal has marked the year in his head. He is already looking forward to going.

fashion_beauty.variant_title: How Fashion Tells Saudi Stories
fashion_beauty.variant_body:
When fashion founder Lujain launched her first collection, she was asked in every interview why she chose the abaya as her medium. The question always surprised her slightly, because for her the answer was so obvious. The abaya is not a limitation — it is a canvas. It is the garment at the centre of Saudi women's daily identity, and it carries more cultural heritage and expressive possibility than almost any other item of clothing in the world.

Lujain grew up watching her mother choose her abaya each morning with the same careful attention that a painter gives to choosing colours. The weight of the fabric, the cut of the sleeve, the small details at the hem — each choice was a form of communication. This woman is serious today. This woman is celebrating. This woman is comfortable. This woman is ready for anything.

Contemporary abaya design in Saudi Arabia has grown rapidly over the past decade. Young designers are combining the traditional silhouette with modern textile technology, sustainable fabrics, and the influence of global fashion trends. The result is a garment that looks simultaneously rooted in heritage and completely of the present moment.

Stylist Reem has worked with several of these designers and finds the creative energy in the Saudi modest fashion sector genuinely exciting. She believes that modest fashion — clothing that covers the body without hiding the personality — is one of the most interesting design challenges in the contemporary industry, because it requires solutions that are both functional and beautiful.

Influencer Hala has used her platform to celebrate Saudi abaya culture to an international audience. She regularly features local designers, explains the cultural significance of different styles and fabrics, and addresses the misconceptions that some international viewers bring to the subject.

"Every garment I wear is a conversation with my history," she wrote in one post that was shared widely outside Saudi Arabia. "The textile of my abaya is woven from generations of women who dressed with intention. I am simply continuing that conversation."

fashion_beauty.variant_title: The Saudi Stylist Who Works from Everywhere
fashion_beauty.variant_body:
Stylist Reem does not have a fixed office. She works from the boutiques of her clients, from the fashion studios of the designers she collaborates with, from coffee shops near her apartment in al-Olaya, and, when she is travelling for work, from hotel rooms and airport lounges across the Gulf and beyond. She has built a career that is entirely remote-compatible, and she considers this one of the most valuable professional decisions she has ever made.

The flexible structure of her work became possible gradually, as the fashion and styling industry moved more of its processes online. Mood boards are shared digitally. Collections can be reviewed through high-resolution photography. Client consultations can be done effectively on video, especially for clients who have worked with Reem long enough to trust her eye. The coworking spaces available in Riyadh, Jeddah, and Dubai provide professional environments for in-person meetings when they are necessary.

The nomadic pattern of her work also suits her creative process. She finds that she generates her best ideas when she is in motion — looking at how women in different cities dress, noticing the colours and materials that dominate different environments, and bringing these observations back to the wardrobe consultations she does for her Saudi clients.

Influencer Hala, who has a similar relationship with location and work, told Reem that the key to maintaining productivity without a fixed routine was to be extremely deliberate about how she structured each day. Hala uses the first hour of every morning for planning: reviewing commitments, setting priorities, and identifying the two or three most important things she wants to accomplish before lunch.

Fashion founder Lujain, who works predominantly from her studio but travels frequently for material sourcing and events, says that the ability to collaborate effectively across distances is one of the most important skills she has developed as her brand has grown. The team that produces her collections is spread across three cities, and keeping them aligned requires both strong digital tools and a genuine culture of trust and communication.

"The work is not the location," Reem says. "The work is the attention you bring to it, wherever you happen to be."

fashion_beauty.variant_title: When a Sandstorm Cancels the Fashion Show
fashion_beauty.variant_body:
The most ambitious fashion event of Lujain's career was a live outdoor show planned for the rooftop terrace of a new cultural centre in Riyadh. She had been preparing for six months. The models had been fitted. The lighting had been designed. The music had been composed specifically for the occasion. And then, forty-eight hours before the event, the weather forecast changed. A sandstorm of significant magnitude was approaching from the west.

The decision to evacuate the rooftop and move the event indoors was not easy. The terrace was the reason Lujain had chosen the venue — the skyline view and the open air were integral to the concept of the collection, which was inspired by the natural landscapes of Arabia. Moving inside would fundamentally change the experience.

But the seismic weather data was clear. The storm was expected to reach full intensity during the exact hours of the planned show. Visibility on the rooftop would be essentially zero. Debris could damage the garments, the equipment, and possibly the guests. There was no realistic alternative.

Lujain made the decision calmly and immediately. She called her team together, explained the situation, and asked everyone to begin rethinking the show for an interior space. What happened next surprised her. Freed from the constraints of the original concept, her team produced something she had not anticipated — a more intimate, more intense version of the show that used the indoor architecture in ways that the rooftop would never have allowed.

The aftershock of the experience was, in a strange way, positive. Lujain wrote about it afterward and said that the best creative decisions of her career had all involved a sudden change of plan. The infrastructure of a well-prepared team, she suggested, is not the ability to execute a fixed plan perfectly — it is the ability to respond to unexpected circumstances with creativity and speed.

Stylist Reem was in the audience on the night of the indoor show. She said it was the most beautiful collection she had seen in years.

fashion_beauty.variant_title: Reading the Codes in Ancient Arabian Jewellery
fashion_beauty.variant_body:
In the collection of a private museum in Jeddah, there is a set of silver jewellery that was found during the excavation of an ancient site in the Hijaz region. The pieces are extraordinary — heavy silver necklaces, elaborate head ornaments, and wide cuffs decorated with geometric patterns and inscriptions. A scholar who specialises in ancient Arabian material culture has been working for two years to interpret the symbols and understand what they meant to the women who wore them.

The process of studying ancient jewellery is similar in many ways to reading a text. Every element of the design carries meaning — the geometric patterns encode information about the community of origin, the symbols indicate social status or marital state, and the inscriptions, where they appear, sometimes contain prayers or dedications. A scholar who knows how to translate these visual languages can read a piece of jewellery almost like a letter from the past.

Stylist Reem attended a lecture by the scholar and was deeply struck by the idea that jewellery has always been a form of communication. She had always felt this intuitively in her work — that the choice of what to wear, and where, and how, is a form of self-expression that carries real meaning. Learning that this had been true in formalised ways for thousands of years gave her intuition a historical foundation.

Fashion founder Lujain has begun incorporating research into ancient Arabian jewellery designs into the accessories she produces alongside her clothing collections. She works with the scholar to ensure that the references she uses are accurate and respectful, and that the evidence from historical pieces is being used thoughtfully rather than simply as decoration.

Influencer Hala featured one of Lujain's historically inspired pieces in a post that drew significantly more engagement than her usual content. Followers were particularly interested in the story behind the piece — where the design came from, what the symbols meant, and how a piece of jewellery worn by a woman two thousand years ago could still feel relevant and beautiful today.

To preserve this knowledge, the scholar says, is one of the most important things anyone can do.

fashion_beauty.variant_title: The Mural That Put Saudi Fashion on the Map
fashion_beauty.variant_body:
On the side of a building in a creative neighbourhood in Riyadh, there is a mural that has been photographed more than any other piece of public art in the city. It shows a group of Saudi women in a range of styles — a young woman in a structured abaya and heels, an older woman in traditional dress with intricate embroidery, a girl in jeans and a headscarf, a woman in a flowing kaftan at a rooftop gathering. They are standing together, looking directly at the viewer, and the colours are extraordinarily vibrant — deep indigo, warm terracotta, gold, and the particular shade of sage green that is found in the highland plants of Asir.

The mural was commissioned by the building's owner, a property developer who had spent time living in cities where street art was an integral part of the visual culture. He contacted a young Saudi artist named Amira, who spent three months preparing the design and three weeks painting it on the wall with a team of assistants.

The response was not entirely simple. Some people were uncomfortable with the scale and public nature of the image. A small number of complaints were made to the neighbourhood council, raising the familiar controversial question of what constitutes appropriate public art and who has the permission to decide.

But the majority response was strongly positive. Stylist Reem visited the mural on the day it was completed and stood looking at it for a long time. She felt that it had done something that fashion photography rarely manages — it had represented the genuine variety of Saudi women's style without exoticising or simplifying it.

Influencer Hala's photograph of the mural was shared more than forty thousand times. Fashion founder Lujain commissioned Amira to create a smaller version of the image for her boutique interior. The mural had, in the simple and ancient way that public art does when it works, transformed not just a building but a conversation about who Saudi women are and how they choose to present themselves to the world.

fashion_beauty.variant_title: What Sleep Does for Your Skin
fashion_beauty.variant_body:
Dr. Mariam sees dozens of patients every week at her skincare clinic in al-Olaya. She treats dehydration, pigmentation, sensitivity, and the cumulative effects of sun exposure on skin that has been living in one of the harshest climates in the world. She uses the most advanced treatments available. But the recommendation she gives to every single patient — the one that is most often ignored and most consistently effective — is also the simplest: sleep more.

During sleep, the body does its most important repair work. Skin cells regenerate, collagen is produced, and the immune processes that fight inflammation and environmental damage are at their most active. When a person is consistently deprived of good sleep, all of these processes are disrupted. The skin loses its ability to function at its optimal level. Fine lines become more visible, the complexion loses its brightness, and the skin barrier becomes weaker and more reactive.

Influencer Hala knows this from personal experience. During a period when she was working very long hours to build her platform, she noticed that her skin was changing. No product was making a difference. She felt overwhelmed and was not sure what to try next. A visit to Dr. Mariam gave her a clear answer: she needed to sleep.

Hala began going to bed at a fixed time and protecting her sleep with the same seriousness she gave to her workout routine and her diet. Within three weeks, the change in her skin was noticeable. Her followers began commenting on her improved complexion before she had even mentioned the change.

Stylist Reem has observed the same pattern in many of the clients she works with. The women who look most consistently radiant are rarely the ones using the most products or following the most complicated routines. They are the ones who sleep well, drink enough water, and manage stress with some degree of success.

"Your skin is trying to tell you something," Dr. Mariam tells her patients. "When it looks tired, it is usually because you are tired. The most honest thing about your face is that it cannot lie."

fashion_beauty.variant_title: The Water Crisis Hiding in Your Beauty Products
fashion_beauty.variant_body:
Dr. Mariam gives her patients a lot of advice about water. Drink more of it, she tells them. Avoid products that disrupt the skin's natural moisture barrier. Use a humidifier in air-conditioned rooms. But the water conversation she has started having more recently is a different one — it is about the extraordinary amount of water that goes into making the beauty products her patients use every day.

The production of cosmetics and personal care products is one of the least-discussed contributors to water scarcity in arid regions. The manufacturing of a single bottle of shampoo, moisturiser, or toner can require many litres of water in processing, cleaning, and testing at various stages of production. In a country where groundwater depletion is already a serious concern, this matters.

Dr. Mariam has begun sourcing a greater proportion of the products she recommends from brands that have made water efficiency a genuine priority. Some of these brands use waterless formulations — concentrated products that require only a small amount of water to activate when the consumer is ready to use them. Others use recycled water throughout their manufacturing process and irrigate their ingredient farms with carefully managed grey water systems.

Fashion founder Lujain has been thinking about the same issue in relation to textile production. The amount of water required to irrigate cotton plants, to process raw fibres, and to dye fabric is extraordinary. Drought conditions in the regions where her fabric suppliers operate are becoming more frequent and more severe, and this is beginning to affect both the quality and the availability of the natural materials she prefers to use.

Influencer Hala made a video about water use in the beauty and fashion industries that generated more controversy than almost anything she had previously produced. Some of her followers felt she was being preachy; others said it was the most important thing she had ever posted.

She stood by it. "The aquifer under the desert," she wrote in her caption, "does not care about your skincare routine. But the scarcity it causes will."

fashion_beauty.variant_title: The Dramatic Landscapes Behind Saudi Jewellery Design
fashion_beauty.variant_body:
When people ask fashion founder Lujain where her jewellery designs come from, she often gives an answer that surprises them. She does not describe other designers who have inspired her, or historical artefacts she has studied, or global fashion trends she has followed. She describes landscapes.

The geology of Saudi Arabia, she explains, is one of the most varied and dramatic on earth. The ancient red sandstone formations of AlUla, the volcanic black rock fields of the Harrat region, the breathtaking terrain of the Asir mountains, the glittering white salt flats near the Gulf coast — each of these landscapes has a visual language, a set of colours and textures and formations that she has tried to translate into jewellery.

Her most celebrated collection was inspired by the ancient lava fields north of Madinah. The pieces were made from oxidised silver and black volcanic stone, with formations of clustered beads that echoed the rough, irregular shapes of cooled lava. The collection was stark and powerful — nothing like the delicate gold work that dominates much of traditional Saudi jewellery. But it felt, to many who saw it, deeply connected to the land.

Stylist Reem describes Lujain's landscape-inspired jewellery as "wearable geology." She believes that this connection to the physical character of the land is what gives the pieces their unusual emotional weight. "When you wear one of these pieces," she told an interviewer, "you are carrying a piece of the terrain with you. And because the terrain is ancient, you are also carrying time."

Influencer Hala wore a piece from the lava collection to a fashion event in Dubai and received more questions about it than about anything she had worn in the previous year. She filmed a short video explaining the geological formation that had inspired the design, and it became one of the most shared pieces of content she had ever produced.

The ancient formations of the Saudi landscape, Lujain believes, are an inexhaustible source of design inspiration — and they are entirely, unmistakably Saudi.

fashion_beauty.variant_title: The Smart Mirror That Knows Your Skin
fashion_beauty.variant_body:
When Dr. Mariam's skincare clinic in al-Olaya upgraded its consultation room last year, the most significant new addition was not a treatment device — it was a mirror. The smart mirror uses sensors to analyse a patient's skin in detail: moisture levels, oil distribution, UV damage, pore size, and surface texture are all measured and displayed on a screen within thirty seconds of a patient sitting down.

The technology allows Dr. Mariam to monitor changes in a patient's skin over time with a precision that was previously only possible in a laboratory. She can optimise her treatment recommendations based on actual data rather than visual inspection alone, and she can show patients exactly what is happening to their skin in a way that is much more convincing than a verbal explanation.

For patients, the experience is revelatory. Many people arrive at the clinic believing that their skincare problems are the result of the wrong products. The skin sensor data often reveals a more complex picture — dehydration layered under excess oil, UV damage from years of sun exposure appearing in patterns that are invisible to the naked eye, and inflammatory processes that have been quietly developing for a long time.

The interface between patient and data is managed carefully. Dr. Mariam is aware that presenting patients with detailed images of their skin can be distressing if it is not done with sensitivity and context. She always explains the data in terms of practical solutions rather than problems, and she is careful to note that what the sensor reveals is the starting point for treatment — not a verdict.

Influencer Hala featured the smart mirror in a video about the future of skincare. The response was enormous. Thousands of comments asked where patients could book a consultation. Several followers said they felt the technology would consume less of their time and money than the trial-and-error approach they had been using to manage their skin.

Stylist Reem visited the clinic out of curiosity and found the experience both informative and reassuring. "For the first time," she said, "I understood my own skin."

fashion_beauty.variant_title: How Saudi Beauty Brands Are Learning to Use Less Water
fashion_beauty.variant_body:
When fashion founder Lujain decided to launch a line of natural skincare products alongside her fashion collection, she quickly discovered that the ingredient that creates the most production complexity is one of the most basic: water. Pure, pharmaceutical-grade water is the base of almost every conventional skincare formulation. In Saudi Arabia, where every litre of clean water requires either desalination or significant treatment, this is not a trivial concern.

Lujain spent several months researching alternative approaches. She found a growing community of cosmetic scientists who specialise in waterless formulations — concentrated serums, balms, oils, and powders that deliver active ingredients without requiring water as a carrier. These products are typically more potent, require smaller packaging, and generate less waste at the manufacturing stage.

She also learned about the water recycling systems used by some of the most advanced cosmetic manufacturers in Europe and Asia. These facilities treat and reuse process water through a closed-loop system, reducing their total water consumption to a fraction of what a conventional factory would require. Efficient water management of this kind is now a standard expectation for brands seeking major international certifications.

Dr. Mariam became an adviser to Lujain's skincare project and contributed her clinical knowledge to the product development process. She was particularly interested in formulations designed for skin that lives in a hot, dry, sun-intense climate — the specific challenges of Saudi skin, which are not well served by products designed primarily for European or American conditions.

The conservation of water in beauty and skincare manufacturing is not a topic that appears often in fashion magazines or influencer content. But Influencer Hala has committed to covering it, and she has found that her followers are more interested than she expected. The supply of information about the environmental impact of their purchasing choices, she has discovered, is something that many people actively want.

"Consumers are not the problem," she wrote in one post. "The problem is the lack of transparent supply chains. Give people good information, and most of them will make good choices."

fashion_beauty.variant_title: The Fashion Documentaries Changing How the World Sees Saudi Women
fashion_beauty.variant_body:
Three years ago, a documentary about Saudi female fashion designers was shown at a film festival in London. The film followed three designers — one working in traditional abaya design, one exploring modest fashion for global markets, and one creating streetwear influenced by Saudi visual culture — over a period of six months. It showed their studios, their creative processes, and the communities around them.

The global response was remarkable. The film reached an audience that had very little previous exposure to Saudi fashion culture, and for many viewers it fundamentally changed what they thought Saudi women's lives looked like. Not because it argued against stereotypes or made any kind of direct claim — but because it simply showed three talented, ambitious, articulate women doing interesting creative work and living full and complex lives.

Influencer Hala saw the documentary and cried. Not from sadness, but from recognition. She felt that the film had captured something true about the experience of being a young Saudi woman interested in fashion — the combination of deep pride in cultural heritage and genuine excitement about the global possibilities of contemporary design.

She wrote a long post about the documentary that was read by more than half a million people. She said that the most powerful thing a fashion documentary could do was not to advocate for any particular style or political position, but simply to make its subjects fully visible — to show the world not what they wore, but who they were.

Stylist Reem attended a screening of the film at a cultural centre in Riyadh and stayed for the discussion afterward. She was struck by how many young women in the audience said they had felt inspired to pursue careers in fashion after watching it. The documentary had had a real and measurable impact on the generation it represented.

Dr. Mariam noted that increased confidence and a positive sense of cultural identity have measurable effects on mental and physical health. Fashion, when it is connected to genuine self-expression, is not trivial. It is a matter of genuine wellbeing.

fashion_beauty.canonical_title: Finding Balance in a Digital World
fashion_beauty.variant_title: Finding Balance When Fashion Never Stops
fashion_beauty.variant_body:
Influencer Hala posts seven days a week. Fashion content, skincare routines, outfit choices, product reviews, brand partnerships, and the occasional personal reflection — her feed is always full, always updated, and always generating the engagement that keeps her professional relationships strong and her income stable.

Last year, she realised she had not bought a single item of clothing for herself — for the pure pleasure of owning it — in more than two years. Every purchase was a potential post. Every outfit was content before it was clothing. She had completely lost the boundary between her personal relationship with fashion and her professional one.

She felt overwhelmed in a way she found difficult to explain to people who did not work in her field. The notifications never stopped. The pressure to react to every trend, every new brand, every cultural moment in fashion was relentless. She began to feel that she was no longer a person who loved fashion — she was a machine that processed it.

Stylist Reem noticed the change in Hala before Hala was fully aware of it herself. She suggested a practical experiment: one week without posting anything, without reading any fashion news, without checking what her competitors were doing. Just living.

Hala was resistant. She worried about losing visibility and momentum. But she agreed to try. She switched off all her notifications, told her audience she was taking a break, and spent seven days doing exactly what Reem had suggested.

On the third day, she went shopping alone and bought a dress that she had no intention of photographing. She wore it that evening to a family dinner. Nobody commented on it, nobody photographed it, and she felt completely free.

When she returned to her platform, she was more mindful about the choices she made. She introduced a clear boundary between paid content and personal content, and she began to rebuild the private relationship with fashion — the one that had made her want to share it with the world in the first place. The detox, she said, had saved her career.

fashion_beauty.variant_title: Bringing Traditional Saudi Dyes Back to Life
fashion_beauty.variant_body:
In a small studio on the edge of an old neighbourhood in Riyadh, a textile artist named Nouf is working to restore a dying craft. She is researching and reviving the traditional plant-based dyes that Saudi women once used to colour fabric — deep indigo from dried plants, warm terracotta from mineral-rich earth, and a soft sage green extracted from native desert shrubs that most people have never heard of.

Nouf became interested in this work after reading that many of the synthetic dyes used in modern fashion manufacturing are among the most significant sources of water pollution in the world. She wanted to find an alternative — one that was rooted in local knowledge and local materials — and she found it by talking to elderly women in rural communities who still remembered the old methods.

Fashion founder Lujain visited Nouf's studio and immediately saw the potential. The colours that Nouf was producing were extraordinary — soft, complex, and completely unlike the flat, uniform tones of synthetic dye. They had a quality that Lujain described as living: they seemed to shift slightly in different lights, and they aged beautifully rather than fading badly.

Lujain commissioned a small collection of abayas using Nouf's plant-dyed fabrics. The collection required months of preparation. Native plants had to be sourced sustainably from suppliers who understood the importance of not over-harvesting. The dyeing process itself required careful monitoring — temperature, time, and the mineral content of the water all affected the final colour.

The corridor between traditional knowledge and contemporary fashion, Lujain believes, is one of the most exciting creative spaces in Saudi design. The thrive of a new generation of craftspeople who are learning these old techniques is not just a cultural event — it is also a response to a real need in an industry that is increasingly aware of the environmental damage caused by its own processes.

Stylist Reem wore one of the plant-dyed abayas to a fashion event and was asked about it all evening. "The colour is the story," she told everyone who asked. "And the story is Saudi Arabia."

fashion_beauty.variant_title: Digital Light and the Future of Saudi Fashion Shows
fashion_beauty.variant_body:
The fashion show that fashion founder Lujain presented at the AlUla Heritage Festival last year was unlike anything she had done before. Instead of a conventional runway, she worked with a team of digital artists to create a light projection experience in which her collection was displayed not on models walking a stage, but on the ancient stone walls of a historic outdoor space. The pieces were shown at full scale, illuminated against the rock, moving gently as if worn by invisible figures.

The effect was augmented by a layer of digital animation that responded to the music — as the sound changed, the light shifted, and the garments appeared to breathe and move. Audience members with smartphones could point their cameras at the walls and see additional layers of information: the name of each piece, the materials used, the Saudi cultural references embedded in the design.

The interactive element was particularly successful. Visitors spent much longer looking at individual pieces than they typically do at a conventional runway show, because there was more to discover. Several people photographed the same wall from different angles over the course of an hour, each time finding a new detail they had missed before.

Stylist Reem attended the event and described it as the most beautiful fashion experience she had ever witnessed. "Fashion has always needed a stage," she said, "but it has never had a stage this old."

Influencer Hala filmed extensively throughout the evening and produced a forty-minute documentary about the event that has been watched more than two million times. The heritage of the AlUla site — its ancient stone, its long human history, its quality of silence and scale — gave the fashion pieces a context that a conventional venue could never have provided.

Lujain believes that the festival experience has opened a new direction for her brand. She intends to make site-specific, technology-enabled fashion events a regular part of how she presents her work, using the landscapes and heritage sites of Saudi Arabia as the permanent, magnificent backdrop for contemporary Saudi design.

fashion_beauty.variant_title: Designing for the Saudi Dust Storm
fashion_beauty.variant_body:
Every summer, stylist Reem receives the same questions from her clients. How do you keep your skin protected when the air is full of dust? What fabrics are best when the wind is carrying sand particles? How do you maintain a polished look during the severe weather that sweeps across Riyadh in late spring?

These questions matter because the Saudi climate is genuinely demanding. Sandstorms are a natural phenomenon that occurs several times a year, and they disrupt outdoor plans and affect everything from skin condition to the colours of garments that are left in the car. A wardrobe that does not account for the climate is a wardrobe that will not work.

Reem has developed a set of practical recommendations for dressing well in a sandstorm-prone environment. The first is fabric choice. Fine silks and delicate open-weave textiles look beautiful but are damaged by dust and difficult to clean after exposure to a heavy storm. Tightly woven fabrics — particularly those with a slight natural texture — are far more practical and are also more forgiving of the regional dust.

Dr. Mariam adds a skincare dimension to this conversation. During a severe storm, when the particle density in the air is high, the skin is under significant oxidative stress. Antioxidant serums applied before leaving the house can provide meaningful protection. A light, breathable covering over the face is not just a cultural choice — it is a practical and intelligent response to an environment that attacks the skin barrier.

Influencer Hala made a video about her sandstorm beauty routine that was watched by more than four hundred thousand people. She showed her skincare steps, her fabric choices, and the specific colours she avoids during dust season — pale pinks and whites that show sand immediately — in favour of warm terracottas and deep neutrals that disguise it gracefully.

The visibility of the sandstorm problem in fashion and beauty content, Reem believes, is actually doing something useful: it is making Saudi women more informed, more practical, and more confident in making choices that suit their actual lives.

fashion_beauty.variant_title: How Stylists Build a Memory Palace for Colour
fashion_beauty.variant_body:
Stylist Reem has been working in the fashion industry in al-Olaya for twelve years. She can walk into a client's wardrobe, spend ten minutes looking at what is there, and come out with a complete picture of that person's style history, their colour preferences, and the three or four pieces that are missing from their collection. Her clients often ask how she does it.

"I use a memory palace," she tells them. "Every wardrobe is a location, and every colour is a story."

The memory palace technique is a visual system for organising information by associating it with specific, familiar locations. Reem uses it constantly in her work. When she meets a new client, she begins to encode their style profile by linking different elements — colour, texture, occasion, mood — to different locations in an imaginary boutique that exists only in her mind. The deep blue silks go by the window. The structured black pieces stand near the entrance. The soft neutral tones fill the back room.

When she is selecting pieces for a client, she simply walks through her imaginary boutique and retrieves what she needs. The technique works because the brain is exceptionally good at remembering places and spatial relationships. When information is tied to a familiar visual landscape, it becomes much easier to recall at the right moment.

Influencer Hala tried the technique after Reem described it to her, and was surprised by how quickly it changed the way she thought about her own wardrobe. She started associating each major colour palette in her collection with a different room in her apartment. The result was that she stopped buying pieces that she already owned and started identifying the genuine gaps in her wardrobe.

Fashion founder Lujain uses the technique differently. She uses it to remember the design history of her brand — each collection associated with a specific time and place, encoded as a walk through the city where it was conceived. This helps her maintain a coherent visual identity across many seasons and many individual pieces.

The memory palace, Reem says, is not a trick. It is simply the brain's natural talent for storytelling, put to practical use.

fashion_beauty.variant_title: The Ancient Beauty Secrets Under the Sand
fashion_beauty.variant_body:
When archaeologists began to excavate an ancient trading settlement near AlUla, they expected to find the usual artefacts of daily life — pottery, tools, and traces of food. What they also found, in a chamber that appeared to have been used by women, were the remains of cosmetic materials: ground minerals in shades of green and black, small carved stone containers, and the faint traces of aromatic resin that had been used as perfume.

These discoveries revealed something extraordinary. The women of this ancient civilisation had developed a sophisticated cosmetic practice that was, in many ways, remarkably similar to modern skincare and beauty rituals. They used kohl made from ground minerals to protect and decorate their eyes — a practice that is still followed by many Saudi women today. They used aromatic resins for fragrance. They had containers specifically designed for storing these materials and applying them carefully.

Each layer of excavation added new detail to the picture. The ruin of what appeared to be a small workshop contained grinding stones and mixing bowls that would have been used to prepare cosmetic materials. The civilisation that had inhabited this settlement had access to trade routes that brought minerals and aromatic plants from across the ancient world, and they had used these materials with considerable skill.

Stylist Reem visited the exhibition of finds from the excavation when it opened at a museum in Riyadh. She spent a long time looking at the small stone containers. They were beautiful objects — simple, functional, and clearly made with care. She thought about the women who had used them, who had sat in a hot desert landscape and applied kohl to their eyes with the same attention that her clients bring to their morning routines today.

Influencer Hala was so moved by the exhibition that she dedicated a week of content to the history of beauty practices in ancient Arabia. The response from her followers, she said, was the most enthusiastic of any series she had ever produced. People, it seemed, wanted to understand where their traditions came from.

fashion_beauty.canonical_title: Racing Against Time: Saving Earth's Wildlife
fashion_beauty.variant_title: Saving the Plants That Perfume Our World
fashion_beauty.variant_body:
In the perfume atelier that founder Lujain runs in al-Olaya, several of the most prized ingredients come from plants that are now considered vulnerable in the wild. Oud — the fragrant resinous heartwood of the agarwood tree — is one of the most expensive natural materials in the world, and it is also one of the most threatened. The species that produces it is classified as endangered in many of the countries where it grows, because demand has grown far faster than the trees can naturally reproduce.

Lujain has spent the past three years working to ensure that every ingredient in her fragrances comes from a sustainable and ethical source. She visited farmers in Southeast Asia who are growing agarwood trees on certified plantations, providing a legal and traceable alternative to wild harvesting. She worked with suppliers who protect the habitat of wild oud trees in protected forest areas and who contribute to conservation programmes funded by a portion of each sale.

The issue is not limited to oud. Rose oil, sandalwood, frankincense, and many other natural fragrance ingredients are facing similar pressures. The habitat of these plants is being destroyed by land conversion, climate change, and overharvesting. Some species face the real possibility of becoming so rare in the wild that they could effectively become extinct as a source of natural fragrance within a generation.

Influencer Hala raised this issue with her followers in a post about sustainable beauty. The response was larger than she expected. Many of her followers had not known that their favourite fragrances might contain ingredients that were contributing to the destruction of endangered plants. Several wrote to say that the information had changed the way they thought about what they were buying.

Stylist Reem now asks every perfume brand she works with about the sourcing of their ingredients. Poaching of wild aromatic plants, she tells her clients, is not an abstract environmental issue — it is something that the fragrance industry has the power to stop by choosing where it buys its raw materials. "Every bottle you choose," she says, "is a vote for the world you want to live in."

fashion_beauty.variant_title: How Fashion Films Are Shaping Saudi Style
fashion_beauty.variant_body:
Influencer Hala remembers the exact moment when a fashion documentary changed the way she thought about her work. She was watching a film about the history of haute couture in France — the long workrooms, the thousands of hours of labour, the extraordinary skill required to produce a single garment. She had grown up in a world of fast fashion and rapid trend cycles, and the slow, deliberate attention shown in the film was almost shocking.

After watching it, she spent two weeks reviewing her own content. She looked at the narrative she had been building about fashion — the speed, the volume, the constant pressure to produce something new — and she realised that this narrative was not the one she actually believed in. She had been portraying fashion as a consumer product when she actually experienced it as a form of culture.

The film had shifted her perspective. She began to change the kind of content she produced. Instead of reaction videos to new collections, she started making longer pieces about the stories behind Saudi designers — their processes, their influences, and the specific cultural references they were working with. Instead of outfit posts, she began exploring the history of specific garments and what they represented.

Her audience grew more slowly after this change, but the engagement was significantly deeper. People were reading her longer pieces, sharing them with comments, and asking questions that showed genuine curiosity about fashion as a form of cultural expression.

Stylist Reem believes that fashion documentaries have done more to educate the public about the true value of good design than any amount of magazine journalism or social media posting. They create emotional connection by showing the human effort and intention behind beautiful objects — which is exactly what makes people appreciate those objects more.

Fashion founder Lujain has been thinking about making a short documentary about her own design process. She wants to show viewers how a piece moves from initial inspiration to final garment — the representation of Saudi identity that she tries to embed in every design, and the influence of her heritage on every creative decision she makes.

fashion_beauty.variant_title: The Algorithm That Knows What You Want to Wear
fashion_beauty.variant_body:
Influencer Hala has a complicated relationship with fashion algorithms. On one hand, she understands how they work and uses them expertly to build her audience and find partnerships with brands. On the other hand, she worries about what they do to personal style. When an algorithm decides what content a consumer sees, it also shapes what they believe is beautiful, desirable, and worth buying.

Fashion recommendation algorithms work by analysing what a person has previously bought, saved, or looked at, and using this data to personalise the content and products they are shown. The trend toward algorithmic fashion curation has grown rapidly over the past five years, and most major online retailers now use these systems to drive their sales.

Stylist Reem has a more critical view. She has noticed that algorithm-driven fashion tends to create clusters of very similar style — everyone being pushed toward the same colours, the same silhouettes, the same brand signatures — because the system learns what people like and then shows them more of the same. This can make fashion culture less diverse and less interesting over time.

Fashion founder Lujain built her brand deliberately outside the algorithmic mainstream. She does not sell through the large virtual retail platforms that dominate online fashion. She sells directly from her own website and through a small number of carefully selected boutiques in Riyadh and Jeddah. She invests in human relationships rather than data-driven personalise tools.

The results have been slower to build, but they have been more stable. Her customers are genuinely loyal rather than algorithmically targeted. They come back because they trust her taste, not because a system has learned to push her products to people who look like her previous buyers.

"Fashion is not a product problem," Lujain told a retailer who tried to persuade her to join a major platform. "It is a relationship problem. And relationships are not solved by algorithms."

fashion_beauty.variant_title: The Sustainable Fashion Hub Opening in Riyadh
fashion_beauty.variant_body:
The new fashion and design district being developed in a former industrial area of Riyadh is designed around a single principle: sustainable style is not a compromise — it is an upgrade. Every building in the district will be powered by renewable energy. The urban planning integrates green spaces and pedestrian routes that encourage people to walk between boutiques, ateliers, and creative studios rather than drive. The infrastructure has been designed to be as efficient as possible, using recycled water for landscaping and building materials sourced locally to reduce transportation costs.

Fashion founder Lujain was one of the first designers to commit to opening a studio in the new district. She believes that the physical environment where fashion is created and sold has a direct effect on the quality and character of what is produced. A studio powered by solar energy and surrounded by carefully maintained gardens produces, she says, a different quality of attention than one in a windowless shopping mall.

Stylist Reem has been working with the district's organisers to develop a programme of educational events for fashion students and young designers. She wants the district to be a place of learning as well as commerce — somewhere that the next generation of Saudi fashion talent can come to understand the full scope of what sustainable practice means, from the sourcing of raw materials to the end of a garment's life.

Dr. Mariam, whose skincare clinic will also be located in the district, is planning to host a series of talks on sustainable beauty — exploring the environmental impact of cosmetic ingredients and packaging, and the growing body of evidence that natural and locally sourced ingredients are often as effective as synthetic alternatives.

Influencer Hala has already begun building content around the district's development. She has committed to featuring only designers who meet the district's sustainability criteria, and she has been open with her followers about why she is making this choice.

"Fashion is one of the most polluting industries in the world," she wrote in a recent post. "But it also has more power to change its own behaviour than almost any other industry. The question is whether we choose to use that power."

fashion_beauty.variant_title: A Retreat That Redesigns Your Relationship with Beauty
fashion_beauty.variant_body:
Three times a year, stylist Reem organises what she calls a "style retreat" — a two-day residential workshop held at a small guesthouse in the mountains near Taif. The participants are women from Riyadh and Jeddah who have come to feel disconnected from their own sense of personal style, often after years of following trends, consuming influencer content, and buying clothes that do not truly reflect who they are.

The retreat has no mirrors in the sleeping rooms and no Wi-Fi. Participants are asked to leave their phones in a basket at reception when they arrive. The programme begins with a long conversation about each woman's relationship with clothing — not what she wears, but why. What she is trying to communicate when she chooses an outfit. What she is trying to hide, and what she is trying to show.

On the second morning, Reem takes the group through a wardrobe exercise using only the clothes they brought with them. She asks each woman to lay everything out on her bed and then to describe each item in a single sentence — not what it is, but what it means. The exercise produces more emotion than most people expect.

"Fashion is not a superficial subject," Reem tells the group. "The way you dress is a form of self-knowledge. When you lose touch with your own style, you often lose touch with something more fundamental — your authentic sense of who you are and what you value."

The retreat aims to restore that connection. By the end of the two days, each participant has a clearer sense of what she actually wants her wardrobe to express, and a practical plan for achieving it without buying anything new.

Dr. Mariam has spoken about the retreat with several of her patients. She has noticed that women who attend often return with a different relationship not just to fashion but to their own physical appearance — more accepting, more grounded, and more genuinely comfortable in their own skin than a course of treatments had been able to produce. The contemplation that the retreat allows, she believes, is itself a form of healing.

fashion_beauty.variant_title: The Hidden Beauty Treasures of the Saudi Highlands
fashion_beauty.variant_body:
When fashion founder Lujain made her first research expedition to the Asir highlands, she was not looking for design inspiration. She was looking for plants. Specifically, she was looking for the wild herbs and flowers that had been used for centuries by the women of the mountain communities to make natural cosmetics and perfumes.

The altitude of the Asir region — several peaks rise above three thousand metres — creates a dramatically different ecosystem from the lowland desert that most people associate with Saudi Arabia. The cool, moist conditions support an extraordinary variety of plant life, including many endemic species that grow nowhere else in the world. The biodiversity of the region is one of its most remarkable and least known features.

Lujain spent two weeks moving between mountain villages, speaking to elderly women who still remembered the old practices. She found women who could identify dozens of plants by name and describe their uses with precise detail — this flower for its fragrance, this leaf for its properties as a skin toner, this root ground into a paste for treating dry, cracked skin.

The heritage of plant-based beauty knowledge in these communities was, she found, both extraordinary and fragile. Most of the women who possessed it were in their seventies and eighties. Their daughters and granddaughters, who had grown up with access to commercial cosmetics, had not learned the old skills. Without a deliberate effort to document and preserve this knowledge, it would disappear within a generation.

Lujain returned from the expedition with notebooks full of names, methods, and stories. She also brought back carefully collected samples of the plants she had been shown, which she had tested at a cosmetics laboratory in Riyadh for their active properties. Several of them proved to have genuinely valuable skincare compounds — compounds that could, with appropriate development, become the basis for a new line of scientifically validated natural beauty products.

The mountains had given her something she had not known she was looking for: not just ingredients, but a whole philosophy of beauty rooted in the landscape, the seasons, and the deep knowledge of women who had understood their environment for centuries.

fashion_beauty.variant_title: The Desert Expedition That Inspired a Collection
fashion_beauty.variant_body:
Fashion founder Lujain had been designing for ten years when she decided to do something that none of her colleagues had done: she joined an expedition across a section of the Rub' al Khali — the Empty Quarter — for five days, on foot and by camel, with a guide and two other travellers.

She went not to rest or to have an adventure, but to look. She wanted to understand the visual language of extreme desert — the colours, the textures, the quality of light at different times of day — and she wanted to experience the isolation and endurance that come with moving slowly through a landscape where there is nothing human for hundreds of kilometres in every direction.

The navigation of the desert, she found, required a kind of sustained, total attention that she had never previously experienced. There were no landmarks, no roads, and no screens. There was only the sand, the sky, the changing quality of the light, and the extraordinary visual richness of a landscape that most people assume is simply empty.

She filled four notebooks with observations and drawings. She photographed the same dune at ten different times of day and in ten different conditions of light. She pressed samples of sand into folded paper and noted their colours — the warm rose gold of the morning, the bleached white of midday, the deep amber of late afternoon, the pale silver of moonlight.

When she returned to Riyadh and began working on her next collection, everything she designed was different. The colour palette was new — subtle, layered, and deeply informed by the desert light she had spent five days observing. The textures of the fabrics she chose felt different: she had found that the pieces which made her feel most alive in the desert were the ones that moved with the body rather than against it.

Stylist Reem, when she saw the new collection, said it was the best work Lujain had ever done. Influencer Hala said that wearing one of the pieces felt, somehow, like being outside.

islamic.variant_title: How Islamic Clothing Tells Our Stories
islamic.variant_body:
When student Sarah began preparing for her first Umrah journey, she thought carefully about what she would wear. She chose a modest, comfortable abaya made from natural fabric, and a soft white headscarf that had belonged to her grandmother. Wearing the scarf for the first time in the context of worship felt different from wearing it on an ordinary day. It felt like a statement of identity, a connection to heritage, and an expression of values all at once.

Sheikh Abdulrahman has spoken many times about the role of clothing in Islamic culture. He emphasises that modest dress is not simply a rule to be followed, but an expression of a whole way of understanding the relationship between the inner self and the outer world. The way we dress communicates something about who we are, what we value, and how we wish to be seen.

This is true across many cultures, not just in Islamic tradition. Around the world, heritage clothing — the traditional garments worn at festivals, ceremonies, and important occasions — carries enormous cultural meaning. Contemporary fashion designers often draw on these traditions, combining the colours, fabrics, and textile patterns of their heritage with modern cuts and materials.

Grandfather Abu Khalid remembers the clothing of his childhood with great affection. The thobe his grandfather wore was made from a particular weight of white cotton that is rarely produced now. It was not fashionable in any modern sense, but it was perfectly suited to the climate and the way of life. It expressed, Abu Khalid says, a kind of sustainable relationship between people and the materials available in their environment.

Sarah has become interested in the history of Islamic textiles as a result of her Umrah experience. She has been reading about the history of the kiswa — the embroidered covering of the Kaaba — and the extraordinary skill and devotion that goes into its making. Each section of the kiswa is embroidered by hand using golden thread, and the artisans who produce it see their work not simply as craft, but as a form of worship.

islamic.variant_title: The Scholar Who Works from Everywhere
islamic.variant_body:
Sheikh Abdulrahman's youngest colleague at the mosque, a scholar named Dr. Faris, does not have a fixed office. He teaches two classes at the mosque each week, but most of his work is done remotely — writing articles, reviewing manuscripts for publication, and consulting for Islamic educational institutions in three different countries. He works from his home, from the mosque library, from cafés, and, when he is travelling, from hotel rooms and airport lounges.

This flexible way of working was not something that he planned from the beginning. It developed gradually as digital communication made it easier to collaborate with colleagues and students across long distances without the need for physical proximity. A manuscript can be shared and reviewed in real time. A lecture can be given to an audience of hundreds without anyone leaving their home.

Dr. Faris finds that the nomadic pattern of his work suits his intellectual style. He is most productive when he is free to move between environments. A problem that resists him in one place often resolves itself in another — as if the change of context allows his mind to approach the same question from a different angle.

Student Sarah finds his approach inspiring but also demanding. She asked him once how he maintained productivity when there was no external structure forcing him to work at particular times. He said that self-discipline was the foundation of everything — that a scholar without external deadlines needed to set their own, and to hold themselves to them with the same seriousness they would give to a commitment made to another person.

Grandfather Abu Khalid is interested in the coworking spaces that scholars like Dr. Faris sometimes use — communal working environments that are designed for people who work independently but benefit from being in a shared space. He wonders whether something like this might be developed around Islamic libraries and educational institutions, creating flexible spaces where scholars, students, and practitioners of different disciplines could work and think together.

"Knowledge has always travelled," Sheikh Abdulrahman observed when the idea was discussed in the majlis one evening. "Only the methods of travel change."

islamic.variant_title: When the Earth Shook Near the Holy City
islamic.variant_body:
Grandfather Abu Khalid was twelve years old when he experienced his first earthquake. He was sitting in a small mosque near his hometown when the ground began to move. The magnitude of the tremor was not large, but to a child sitting on a tiled floor, it felt as though the entire world had shifted on its foundations. The prayer mats rippled, the water in the ablution pool trembled, and everyone in the room fell very still.
