Wijdan found shelter inside a small restaurant near the market. The owner was a quiet woman who had been running the place for twenty years. She said that sandstorms like this one always disrupt business, but she had learned not to worry. "We make extra soup on dusty days," she said with a calm smile. "People always come in from the storm, and they always want something hot."

The soup she served was a thick lentil broth with cumin and dried lime — exactly the kind of warm, comforting food that Wijdan needed. She sat by the window and watched the dust blow through the empty streets while she ate.

When the storm finally passed, she went back to the market. Most of the stalls had not yet reopened. But the fish sandwich seller was already back, grilling over a small fire. He had not let the storm stop him for long.

Wijdan wrote about the experience in her blog. She said that sandstorms are an unavoidable part of life in the Gulf region, and that they have shaped food culture in ways that people rarely notice. Hot soups, covered dishes, and simple hearty meals are all part of a food tradition built around the reality of desert weather.

travel_food.variant_title: Your Amazing Memory Palace in the Kitchen
travel_food.variant_body:
Chef Hatem has worked in restaurants across al-Olaya for fifteen years. He can remember hundreds of recipes without writing them down. When people ask how he does it, he always smiles and says, "I use a memory palace."

A memory palace is a technique that has been used by scholars and storytellers for thousands of years. The idea is simple but powerful. You choose a familiar place — your home, your favourite restaurant, or the road to Souq Tayba — and you use it to encode information in a visual way.

Here is how Chef Hatem does it. When he wants to remember a new recipe, he associates each ingredient with a different location inside his kitchen. He might place the saffron next to the stove, the dried lime near the window, and the cardamom beside the coffee pot. Each location holds one piece of information, and because the kitchen is so familiar to him, the ingredients are easy to recall.

Food blogger Wijdan tried this technique when she was preparing for a food history presentation about traditional Saudi dishes. She needed to remember facts about kabsa, mutabbaq, and jarish. She walked through her grandmother's house in her mind and placed each dish in a different room. The kabsa was in the majlis, the mutabbaq was on the kitchen table, and the jarish was in the garden. When the presentation day came, she simply walked through the house in her imagination and found everything she needed.

Traveller Mishal used the same technique before a trip to AlUla. He had to remember the names of twelve ancient sites. He associated each name with a location along the road from Riyadh. Every landmark on the highway became a visual trigger for a new piece of information.

Scientists who study memory say this technique works because the human brain is very good at remembering places and images. When you encode information in a visual and spatial way, it becomes much easier to retrieve later. The memory palace connects new knowledge to familiar experiences, and this makes it stronger.

You do not need a real palace to use this method. Any familiar place will work. Your family home, the path through Carrefour, or even the road between Taif and the valley below — all of these can become memory palaces. The technique is flexible, and with a little practice, almost anyone can learn to use it well.

Chef Hatem now teaches the technique to his apprentices at the restaurant. He tells them that a good cook needs not only good hands, but also a well-organised mind. The memory palace, he says, is simply the best way to keep that mind in order.

travel_food.variant_title: The Ancient Kitchens Buried Under the Sand
travel_food.variant_body:
When archaeologists began to excavate the ruins of an ancient settlement near AlUla, they expected to find pottery, tools, and perhaps some artefacts from daily life. What they did not expect to find was an ancient kitchen — complete with stone grinding tools, clay storage jars, and the charred remains of a meal that had been cooked more than two thousand years ago.

The discovery was extraordinary. Each layer of soil revealed new information about the people who had lived there. The civilisation that built this settlement had been skilled farmers and traders. They had grown wheat, kept goats, and traded spices with communities across the ancient world.

The food remains that were found in the excavation told a rich and detailed story. Scientists were able to identify the ruin of grain stores, the remnants of dried fruit, and the traces of aromatic herbs. These findings suggested that the people of this ancient community had eaten a diet that was surprisingly similar in some ways to traditional Saudi food today.

Traveller Mishal visited the site during one of the public excavation days organised by the local heritage authority. He walked along the exposed stone walls and looked down into the deep trenches where researchers were carefully removing layer after layer of compacted earth. A guide explained that each layer represented a different period in the settlement's history, and that the kitchen area had been used continuously for more than five hundred years.

What moved Mishal most was the thought that food had connected people across all of those centuries. The same act of grinding grain, cooking meat, and sharing a meal around a fire — repeated again and again across thousands of years — was the thread that joined these ancient people to the families eating kabsa together in Riyadh today.

He wrote about the experience in his travel journal and shared it with his followers online. The response was remarkable. Many people said that the story made them feel proud of their region's long and rich food history.

travel_food.canonical_title: Racing Against Time: Saving Earth's Wildlife
travel_food.variant_title: Racing to Save the Flavours of the Wild
travel_food.variant_body:
When traveller Mishal visited the highlands near Taif, he noticed something that worried him. The wild herbs and plants that his grandmother used to collect for cooking were becoming very hard to find. The rocky slopes where she once gathered wild thyme and mountain flowers were quiet and almost empty.

Mishal started asking questions. He learned that many of the wild species that once grew across the region are now endangered. Their habitat has been damaged by construction, overgrazing, and climate change. Some plants have disappeared so completely that local cooks can no longer use them in traditional recipes. If nothing changes, these flavours could become extinct forever.

This is a problem that affects food culture deeply. Traditional dishes like kabsa and jarish depend on specific herbs and spices that grow in particular regions of Saudi Arabia. When the habitat of these plants is destroyed, the knowledge of how to use them in cooking is also lost. Conservation of wild plants is not only an environmental issue — it is also a cultural and culinary one.

Food blogger Wijdan has written several articles about this connection between nature and food. She interviewed farmers in Qassim who have started growing endangered herb varieties on their land to protect them. She spoke to cooks in al-Olaya who are changing their menus to support local and sustainable ingredients. She also visited a small mountain farm near Taif where a family has been working for three generations to preserve the wild flavours of their region.

Poaching of rare plants is also a serious problem. Some wild herbs and roots are sold illegally in markets because they are rare and expensive. This makes the problem worse, because when collectors take the last plants from a wild area, there is nothing left to reproduce.

Mishal came home from his trip with a new purpose. He began writing to local authorities and sharing information on social media about the importance of protecting wild plant species. He believes that if people understand the connection between conservation and food culture, they will care more about protecting the natural world.

"We cannot cook our heritage if we destroy the ingredients," he wrote in one post. Many people agreed. The response was stronger than he expected.

travel_food.variant_title: How Food Documentaries Shape the Way We Eat
travel_food.variant_body:
Food blogger Wijdan watches at least one food documentary every week. She has watched films about spice traders in old Jeddah, street food vendors in Tokyo, cheese makers in France, and the history of the Saudi coffee ceremony. Each film, she believes, has changed the way she thinks about food.

A well-made food documentary does more than show you what people eat. It changes the narrative around food culture. It challenges stereotypes about which cuisines are considered important and which are ignored. It shapes how viewers think about the relationship between food, land, identity, and justice.

Traveller Mishal noticed this influence on his own travels. After watching a documentary about the food heritage of AlUla, he found himself paying attention to things he would previously have ignored — the way a vendor arranged his dates on a market stall, the specific herbs hanging to dry above a doorway, the sound of a stone mortar being used in a kitchen somewhere he could not see.

The film had changed his perspective completely. It had made him see food not as a product to consume but as a form of cultural expression that required attention, respect, and curiosity.

Chef Hatem has a more complicated view. He believes that food documentaries are powerful but can also create misleading portrayals of food culture. When a filmmaker travels to a region and chooses which traditions to show and which to ignore, they are making editorial decisions that influence how millions of people around the world will understand that culture. Sometimes those decisions are made in the interest of a good story rather than an accurate representation.

"A film can make a cuisine look romantic or primitive," he said during an interview. "Both portrayals can be equally false."

Despite this concern, Hatem agrees that the best food documentaries have done something important: they have made people curious about food cultures that were previously invisible to them. For food writers like Wijdan, and food travellers like Mishal, this curiosity is the beginning of everything.

travel_food.variant_title: How Technology Is Changing the Way We Discover Food
travel_food.variant_body:
Food blogger Wijdan used to find new restaurants the old-fashioned way. She asked friends, read printed guides, and walked through neighbourhoods until she found something interesting. Now, she mostly uses apps. An algorithm tells her what restaurants she might enjoy based on what she has eaten before, and most of the time, it is right.

This is the digital food revolution. Technology has completely changed the way consumers discover, choose, and experience food. From virtual restaurant tours to personalised menus, the food industry is being transformed by data and software.

Wijdan has mixed feelings about this change. On one hand, she loves the convenience. She can find a hidden food truck in a new neighbourhood in seconds. She can read hundreds of reviews before she visits a restaurant. She can personalise her order before she even arrives — choosing her preferred level of spice, her preferred portion size, and her preferred table.

On the other hand, she worries about what is lost. When an algorithm decides what you eat, you stop discovering things by accident. You stop walking into an unfamiliar street and being surprised by a smell coming from an open kitchen. You stop making the happy mistakes that lead to the best food memories.

Chef Hatem has a different perspective. He sees technology as a tool, not a replacement for real food culture. His restaurant uses a smart ordering system that allows customers to personalise their meals in detail. Retailer partnerships help him source ingredients efficiently and reduce waste. He believes that if technology is used wisely, it can make food better — not worse.

Traveller Mishal has noticed that some of the best food experiences of his life happened when he ignored the algorithm and followed his instincts. The fish sandwich near Souq Tayba, the lentil soup during the sandstorm, the warm bread from Abu Khalid's bakery — none of these were recommended by an app. They were found by being present, paying attention, and being open to surprise.

Perhaps the best approach is a combination: use technology to plan and prepare, but leave space for the unexpected moments that no algorithm can predict.

travel_food.variant_title: The Food Markets of Tomorrow's Cities
travel_food.variant_body:
When city planners in Riyadh designed the new al-Olaya food district, they had one clear goal in mind: to create a space that was both sustainable and alive. They wanted a market that would reduce waste, use renewable energy, and integrate traditional Saudi food culture with modern life.

The result is a market unlike any other in the region. Every stall is connected to a central system that monitors food freshness and tracks sales in real time. When a vendor's stock is running low, the system sends an alert. When produce is approaching the end of its fresh period, the price is automatically reduced to encourage sales and reduce waste.

The market's infrastructure was designed to be as efficient as possible. Solar panels on the roof provide electricity for the refrigeration units and the lighting. Rainwater is collected and used to clean the floors and water the herb gardens that grow along the market's outer walls. An urban composting system turns food waste into fertiliser that is sent back to the farms that supply the market.

Chef Hatem visits the market every Tuesday morning to choose his ingredients for the week. He loves the combination of traditional produce and modern systems. He can find saffron, dried lime, black seed, and imported spices in the same place where he can also speak to the farmer who grew his vegetables and learn exactly when they were harvested.

Food blogger Wijdan has written extensively about the market's design. She is particularly interested in how it has managed to integrate food culture into the fabric of city life. Unlike a supermarket like Carrefour, where food is anonymous and disconnected from its origins, this market makes the relationship between producer and consumer visible and meaningful.

"This is what sustainable urban food looks like," she wrote in one of her most popular articles. "Not just recycling bins and solar panels — but a genuine connection between the land, the farmer, the cook, and the person eating."

The market has become one of the most visited places in the city, not just for shopping, but for the experience of being in a space where food is treated with genuine respect.

travel_food.variant_title: Finding the Authentic Meal Far from the City
travel_food.variant_body:
Traveller Mishal had been eating in restaurants along the al-Olaya strip for three weeks when he decided he needed a break. The food was good — sometimes excellent — but something was missing. Every meal felt like a performance, carefully designed for people like him: educated, connected, slightly nostalgic for traditions they have never fully lived.

He wanted something authentic. He booked two nights at a small traditional guesthouse in the hills east of Taif, far from any city or major road. The guesthouse had no Wi-Fi, no television, and no printed menus. Meals were served at fixed times in a shared dining room, and the food was whatever the cook had prepared that day.

On the first evening, he sat down to a meal of slow-cooked lamb, homemade bread, dried figs, and a thick yoghurt that tasted completely unlike anything from a supermarket. The guesthouse cook was a quiet woman in her sixties who had been feeding guests for decades without the benefit of recipe apps or food delivery services.

After dinner, Mishal sat in the courtyard and felt something he had not felt in a long time: genuine stillness. The retreat from the city, the disconnect from digital life, and the simple act of eating food that had been cooked with real care and no commercial ambition — all of this produced a kind of contemplation that he had been looking for without knowing it.

He wrote in his journal that evening. He noted that the most nourishing meals he had ever eaten were not in expensive restaurants, but in places where food was connected to tradition and made by someone who cooked not for reviews, but for the pleasure of feeding people well.

He thought about Chef Hatem and his modern smart kitchen, and about Wijdan and her hundreds of thousands of followers. Both were talented and sincere. But there was something about this quiet place, this simple meal, and this woman who had never needed to restore her relationship with food because she had never lost it, that touched him more deeply than anything he could write about online.

travel_food.variant_title: The Mountain Food Treasures of Saudi Arabia
travel_food.variant_body:
When most people think of Saudi food, they think of the flat, sandy desert. But the Asir region in the southwest is a completely different landscape — a world of high mountains, green terraces, and rich biodiversity that produces some of the most distinctive ingredients in the country.

Traveller Mishal had heard about the mountain food culture of Asir for years before he finally made the expedition. He drove south from Riyadh over two days, and when he reached the higher altitudes, he was immediately struck by how different the ecosystem felt. The air was cooler, the vegetation was dense, and the roadside markets sold vegetables and herbs he had never seen before.

The mountain villages of Asir have a food heritage that is largely unknown outside the region. The local diet is based on sorghum, honey, wild herbs, and a variety of dried meats and legumes that have been part of the culinary tradition for centuries. The endemic varieties of honey produced in this region — particularly the famous Sidr honey — are considered among the finest in the world.

Mishal visited a farm where three generations of a family had been keeping bees and producing honey using traditional methods. The elderly grandfather, Abu Saeed, explained that each flower that grows in the mountain ecosystem produces a honey with a different flavour, colour, and texture. The honey from wild Sidr trees, he said, is dark and thick and has a warm, complex taste that no industrial honey can replicate.

He offered Mishal a spoonful directly from the comb. It was one of the most extraordinary flavours he had ever experienced — deeply sweet, slightly bitter, and carrying with it the smell of the mountain wind.

Mishal ate at a small local restaurant that evening where the menu featured dishes that were entirely unfamiliar to him. He recognised some of the grains and spices, but the combinations and techniques were new. The food was grounded in a very specific landscape, and eating it there, in that place, at that altitude, made it taste completely right.

He came home from the trip wanting to write not just about the food, but about the landscape that had created it — because without the mountains, the biodiversity, and the ancient knowledge of the people who lived there, none of it would be possible.

travel_food.variant_title: The Great Saudi Desert Adventure
travel_food.variant_body:
Traveller Mishal had been planning the expedition for eight months. He wanted to cross a section of the Empty Quarter — the largest sand desert in the world — on foot and by camel, carrying only what he needed and stopping at the few desert camps and water points along the route.

The journey would test his endurance in ways that no city experience could prepare him for. The extreme heat during the day, the cold nights, the physical effort of walking across deep sand, and the isolation from any form of modern communication were all challenges he knew he would face. But the experience he was most looking forward to was the food.

Desert travel, he had read, produces an extraordinary relationship with eating. When food is scarce and preparation is difficult, every meal becomes significant. A cup of tea and a piece of dried bread in a desert camp tastes like a gift. A bowl of warm rice after a long day's walk produces a gratitude that is impossible to feel in a restaurant.

He carried dates, dried meat, rice, and coffee. The dates were from a farm in Qassim that he had visited the previous year — the same farm where a farmer had shown him the connection between the soil and the flavour of the fruit. Eating those dates in the desert, far from any road or building, Mishal felt the connection more strongly than ever.

His navigation across the desert relied on the stars at night and on the landmarks and patterns of the dunes during the day. At each camp, he met desert guides and tribal families who welcomed him with coffee and dates in the traditional way. Their hospitality was extraordinary, and each encounter was a lesson in the food customs of desert life.

The expedition ended after twelve days. Mishal arrived at a small settlement on the edge of the desert, sat down in a simple café, and ordered a full meal. He said it was the best he had ever eaten.
